sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Cardiac arrhythmias are abnormal heart rhythms that occur when the electrical signals that coordinate the heart's beats do not work properly. The normal heart rate is usually between 60 and 100 beats per minute. These disturbances can cause the heart to beat too fast (tachycardia), too slow (bradycardia), or irregularly. Arrhythmias can be harmless or life-threatening, depending on their type and severity. Management strategies depend on the type and severity of the arrhythmia, including pharmacologic therapy, electrical cardioversion, or electrical pacing as examples.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Unstable Bradycardia** (HR <60 bpm):
  - Signs: Hypotension (<90 mmHg), altered mental status, cool/pale skin, poor perfusion
  - Symptoms: Dizziness, chest pain, shortness of breath, fatigue, syncope

**Unstable Tachycardia** (HR >100 bpm, often >150 bpm):
  - Signs: Hypotension (<90 mmHg), signs of shock, altered mental status
  - Symptoms: Chest pain, palpitations, shortness of breath, syncope

**Supraventricular Tachycardia (SVT)**:
  - Regular, narrow complex tachycardia
  - Symptoms: Palpitations, lightheadedness, chest discomfort

**Atrial Fibrillation (AF)**:
  - Irregular, narrow complex tachycardia
  - Symptoms: Palpitations, fatigue, breathlessness, dizziness

**Broad Complex Tachycardia** (suspected VT):
  - Regular, wide complex tachycardia - treat as VT until proven otherwise

**Torsades de Pointes**:
  - Polymorphic VT, associated with prolonged QT interval

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- All rhythm-management decisions should be made within scope and with early senior clinical support when required
- Perform a structured assessment including haemodynamic status, symptom severity, likely cause, medication history, and a 12 lead ECG where available
- Bradycardia and tachycardia may be caused by ACS, hypovolaemia, sepsis, toxicology, electrolyte disturbance, endocrine disease, environmental exposure, or structural heart disease
- Treat the patient, not the monitor. A stable patient may still have a dangerous rhythm, and an unstable patient may require urgent electricity rather than repeated medication trials
- Anti-arrhythmic treatments, including vagal manoeuvres, drugs, cardioversion, and pacing, can worsen rhythm or haemodynamics if used in the wrong setting

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Continuous reassurance and minimise exertion
- Perform repeated vital signs and rhythm assessment
- Oxygen only if clinically indicated
- Apply ECG monitoring where available
- Consider Secondary Survey
- Escalate early if the patient is hypotensive, has chest pain, syncope, heart failure, or altered conscious state

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Obtain a 12 lead ECG
- Gain vascular access
- Consider appropriate analgesia or antiemetic if required

**Unstable bradycardia:**
  - Address reversible causes first
  - Manage hypovolaemia, hypoxia, ACS, and toxins as indicated
  - Consider atropine and or pacing in consultation with the on-call doctor
  - If inferior or right ventricular MI with hypotension is suspected, consider cautious fluids and senior clinical input early

**Unstable tachycardia:**
  - Treat as peri-arrest, apply defibrillation pads, and prepare for synchronised cardioversion with appropriate sedation where feasible

**Regular narrow-complex tachycardia:**
  - If unstable, perform synchronised cardioversion
  - If stable, begin with vagal manoeuvres, ideally modified Valsalva where appropriate
  - If vagal manoeuvres fail and the rhythm remains regular and narrow-complex, consider adenosine in consultation with the on-call doctor

**Irregular narrow-complex tachycardia:**
  - Identify and treat contributing causes such as sepsis, ACS, hypoxia, pain, hypovolaemia, or electrolyte disturbance
  - IV fluids may be appropriate when hypovolaemia is suspected, but should not be used as routine rhythm control
  - Consider rate control or anti-arrhythmic therapy only in consultation with the on-call doctor and local protocol

**Regular broad-complex tachycardia:**
  - Treat as VT until proven otherwise
  - If unstable, perform synchronised cardioversion
  - If not immediately cardioverted or if cardioversion fails, consider amiodarone in consultation with the on-call doctor

**Torsades de pointes:**
  - Consider magnesium sulphate in consultation with the on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Resuscitation equipment should be at hand for any of the above presentations
- Consideration should be made that any unstable patient is peri-arrest and time critical.
- ESO’s should have a very low threshold for calling for help
- Clinical deterioration is likely
- Adenosine has a very short half-life. It should be administered rapidly, via a 3-way tap, followed by a saline flush, through a large bore IV (18 – 14g) as close to the heart as practicable, such as the cubital fossa
